enrichment.seo_title: Estuary Flow Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Estuary Flow review: real-time CDC and streaming ETL platform with 200+ connectors, exactly-once delivery, and usage-based pricing. See features, plans, alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Estuary Flow is a unified, right-time data integration platform built to move data continuously between operational databases, SaaS applications, message queues, and analytical destinations without forcing teams to choose between batch ETL and real-time streaming. The product is built by Estuary Technologies, Inc., a company that positions Flow as a single dependable system for change data capture (CDC), streaming pipelines, and traditional batch ETL, arguing that most organizations end up stitching together separate tools for each of these needs and that Flow removes that fragmentation. At its core, Flow lets users define captures, which pull data continuously from a source system such as a Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, or Oracle database, a SaaS API like Salesforce, HubSpot, or NetSuite, or a streaming source like Kafka, Kinesis, or Google Pub/Sub. That captured data lands as durable, schematized collections, which are then transformed using either streaming SQL or TypeScript derivations, or downstream dbt models once landed in a warehouse. Finally, materializations push the resulting data into destinations such as Snowflake, Google BigQuery, Amazon Redshift, Databricks, Elasticsearch, DynamoDB, Pinecone, or back into another operational database. This capture-transform-materialize model is expressed through declarative Flow specifications, giving teams a version-controllable, testable definition of an entire data pipeline rather than a black-box UI-only workflow.

The company's origin story stretches back well before Estuary Technologies was formally founded. Co-founders Dave Yaffe (CEO) and Johnny Graettinger (CTO) met while working at Google, where they operated high-volume, privacy-sensitive data systems during the early rollout of GDPR compliance requirements. In 2014 they left Google to start Arbor, a people-based data marketplace, and it was at Arbor that they began building Gazette, an internal streaming broker designed to store data durably as ordinary files in cloud object storage rather than relying on a traditional in-memory or disk-bound broker like Kafka. LiveRamp acquired Arbor in 2016, and Yaffe and Graettinger continued developing and operating Gazette inside LiveRamp, where by 2017 it was reportedly handling billions of events and multiple gigabytes per second of throughput in production. Drawing on that decade of real-time infrastructure experience, the pair founded Estuary Technologies in 2019 to turn Gazette into the foundation of a general-purpose, customer-facing data movement product. Flow's self-service SaaS product launched in 2023, giving users a hosted UI and cloud control plane on top of the underlying open-core engine. The company raised a Series A round of roughly seven million dollars from FirstMark in 2021, and in 2025 announced an additional seventeen million dollar raise led by M13, with participation from FirstMark and Operator Partners, bringing total disclosed funding to roughly the mid-twenty-million-dollar range. The 2025 round was explicitly earmarked to expand engineering, product, and go-to-market teams and to position Estuary as infrastructure for feeding fresh, dependable data into AI and analytics workloads, reflecting the company's messaging shift toward being a 'right-time data platform for the AI era.'

Technically, Flow's architecture is decoupled into a streaming compute layer and a storage layer. Captured data is durably stored as immutable, ordered collections directly in the customer's or Estuary's cloud object storage (Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, or Azure Blob Storage), which is the same pattern Gazette pioneered at Arbor and LiveRamp: rather than retaining data only in an ephemeral broker cluster, Gazette treats cloud storage itself as the durable, infinitely retained log. Elastic streaming compute nodes handle brokering, transformation, and replay on top of these collections, scaling horizontally and load-balancing automatically. Estuary advertises exactly-once processing semantics and deterministic recovery, meaning a pipeline can be replayed or recovered after a failure without producing duplicate or missing records downstream. This architecture is what allows Flow to serve both sub-100 millisecond streaming use cases and traditional scheduled batch loads from the same underlying pipeline definitions, rather than requiring separate systems for each latency tier. Schema management is largely automated: Flow infers schemas from source data, tracks schema evolution over time, and propagates compatible changes through the pipeline, reducing the operational burden of manually maintaining schema mappings as upstream systems change. The platform ships with more than two hundred pre-built, no-code connectors spanning databases, SaaS applications, event streams, and files, and it maintains compatibility with the Kafka protocol so existing Kafka consumers and producers can interoperate with Flow collections. Much of the platform and connector code is available publicly on GitHub under the estuary/flow repository, released under a Business Source License that permits viewing and self-managed use outside of Estuary's own commercial managed offering, making Flow best described as open-core rather than fully open source.

Estuary's pricing is structured around three tiers. The Developer tier is free forever and includes up to ten gigabytes of data movement per month and up to two concurrent connectors, giving individuals and small teams a genuine no-cost way to try CDC and streaming pipelines with the same connector catalog as the paid Cloud tier, just within stricter limits. The Cloud tier removes those caps and is billed on a usage basis: roughly fifty cents per gigabyte of data moved, plus one hundred dollars per month per connector used, with discounts advertised for customers running six or more connectors, and a thirty-day free trial available before billing begins. Cloud-tier customers get access to the full connector catalog, unlimited users, both millisecond-latency streaming and scheduled batch delivery, role-based access control, the ability to bring their own cloud storage bucket for collection data, choice of US or EU processing regions, SSH tunnel support for private databases, dbt Cloud integration, Kafka-protocol compatibility, and real-time pipeline monitoring and alerting, with standard support delivered through community Slack and email. The Enterprise tier moves to custom, negotiated annual contracts and layers on volume-based pricing discounts, SOC 2 and HIPAA compliance documentation, single sign-on, custom SLA terms, private and bring-your-own-cloud (BYOC) deployment models, dedicated provisioned infrastructure, custom region support, migration assistance from incumbent tools, custom connector development, and dedicated Slack- and email-based support.

Estuary Flow's target users are data engineering and platform teams at growth-stage and mid-market to enterprise companies that need to move data out of transactional databases and SaaS systems into warehouses, lakehouses, or operational stores faster than a typical scheduled-batch ELT tool allows, without hand-rolling a Kafka-and-Debezium stack themselves. It is commonly positioned for use cases such as powering near-real-time analytics dashboards, replicating production databases into a warehouse for reporting without burdening the source system, feeding fresh, right-time context into AI applications and retrieval systems (with native materializations into vector stores like Pinecone), and general operational data synchronization between SaaS tools and internal systems. Publicly referenced customers include Headset, a cannabis-industry analytics company that switched from Airbyte to Estuary and reported cutting Snowflake data ingestion costs by around 40 percent, and Glossier, the beauty retailer, which is cited as having cut data movement costs by roughly half while unlocking real-time supply chain and marketing analytics. On review platforms like G2, users highlight fast time-to-value (some report connecting a source like MongoDB to a warehouse like Snowflake within hours), predictable usage-based pricing, and responsive support, while some reviewers note that observability into running data streams could be improved.

In the competitive landscape, Estuary Flow is most often compared against Fivetran and Airbyte, the two dominant managed ELT platforms, as well as against Confluent (Kafka as a managed service), Debezium (the open-source CDC library many custom pipelines are built on), Meroxa, Striim, and Rivery. Estuary differentiates itself primarily on the claim of being the only platform that unifies continuous streaming CDC, batch ETL, and in-flight transformations into one system with exactly-once guarantees, rather than treating streaming as a bolt-on to a fundamentally batch-oriented product, which is how it characterizes Fivetran's and Airbyte's scheduled-sync architectures. Its usage-based pricing model, rather than Fivetran's monthly-active-rows model, is also a frequent point of comparison in the company's own marketing and third-party comparison writeups. Since its 2023 self-service launch, Estuary has continued to expand its connector catalog past two hundred integrations, added BYOC and private deployment options aimed at security-conscious enterprise buyers, pursued SOC 2 and HIPAA compliance to support regulated industries, and increasingly framed its product around powering AI and LLM pipelines with fresh, dependable data, reflecting the broader market shift toward retrieval-augmented generation and real-time AI applications that require low-latency data movement rather than nightly batch loads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Estuary Flow is a real-time data integration platform that unifies change data capture (CDC), streaming ETL, and batch ELT into a single managed system. Instead of maintaining separate tools for real-time and batch data movement, teams define one pipeline in Flow that can deliver data with sub-100 millisecond latency or on a scheduled batch cadence, depending on the destination's needs.

The platform is built by Estuary Technologies, founded in 2019 by Dave Yaffe and Johnny Graettinger, who previously built the Gazette streaming broker while running Arbor, a data company acquired by LiveRamp in 2016. Flow's architecture stores captured data durably in cloud object storage and uses elastic streaming compute for brokering, transformation, and replay, enabling exactly-once delivery and deterministic recovery.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Flow pipelines are composed of three building blocks: captures, which continuously pull data from a source; derivations, which transform data in-flight using streaming SQL or TypeScript; and materializations, which push transformed data into a destination system. This model is defined declaratively through Flow specifications, making pipelines version-controllable and testable.

Estuary ships more than 200 pre-built connectors covering databases (Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, Oracle), SaaS applications (Salesforce, HubSpot, NetSuite), event streams (Kafka, Kinesis, Google Pub/Sub), and destinations such as Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Databricks, Elasticsearch, DynamoDB, and Pinecone. The platform maintains Kafka-protocol compatibility, automated schema inference and evolution, and role-based access control, and it supports public, private, and bring-your-own-cloud (BYOC) deployment models.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Estuary offers a free-forever Developer tier limited to 10GB of data movement per month and two concurrent connectors, giving individuals and small teams a genuine way to evaluate CDC and streaming pipelines at no cost.

The paid Cloud tier is usage-based, priced at roughly $0.50 per gigabyte of data moved plus $100 per connector per month, with volume discounts for teams running six or more connectors and a 30-day free trial. The Enterprise tier moves to custom annual contracts and adds SOC 2 and HIPAA compliance documentation, single sign-on, private and BYOC deployment, dedicated support, and custom connector development.

enrichment.short_description: EtherCalc is a free, open-source, real-time collaborative web spreadsheet built on Node.js, descended from Dan Bricklin's WikiCalc and Socialtext's SocialCalc engine, that lets anyone share a URL and edit a spreadsheet together instantly.
enrichment.seo_title: EtherCalc Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: EtherCalc review: the open-source, self-hostable real-time spreadsheet born from Dan Bricklin's WikiCalc. See its features, architecture, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
EtherCalc is an open-source, browser-based spreadsheet application built for real-time, multi-user collaborative editing. Its defining idea is simple: a spreadsheet becomes a shared room reachable by URL, and anyone with the link can view or edit it, with every keystroke, cell change, and cursor movement broadcast instantly to every other participant in that room, in the same spirit as the collaborative text editor Etherpad but applied to tabular, formula-driven data instead of prose.

The project's lineage traces back to Dan Bricklin, the co-creator of VisiCalc, the software widely credited as the original electronic spreadsheet. In the mid-2000s Bricklin built WikiCalc, an early attempt to merge the wiki model of open, saved-history editing with spreadsheet functionality. WikiCalc's server-side Perl implementation required a full network round trip for every calculation, which made real-time collaboration impractical. Socialtext, a workplace collaboration software company, picked up the idea and, working with Bricklin, rebuilt it as SocialCalc starting around 2006. The critical architectural shift in SocialCalc was moving the spreadsheet engine itself out of the server and into client-side JavaScript, on the insight that JavaScript execution in the browser, even though slower per-operation than server-side Perl, was still faster overall than paying network latency on every cell recalculation. SocialCalc became part of the Socialtext enterprise collaboration platform and, notably, was released as open-source software.

EtherCalc itself was created by Audrey Tang, who had been involved with the WikiCalc and SocialCalc lineage and who, in Tang's own words, used the project as a personal experiment from mid-2011 into late-2012 to test whether Node.js was mature enough for production use. The GitHub repository for the project (audreyt/ethercalc) was created on August 20, 2011, and is described in its own metadata as a 'Node.js port of Multi-user SocialCalc.' Tang rewrote the SocialCalc server logic first from Perl toward JavaScript, then experimented across CoffeeScript, Coco, and ultimately settled on LiveScript, a language Tang favored for features like backcalls and cascades that avoided deeply nested callback code, which was a common pain point in early asynchronous Node.js applications. An early WebSocket prototype used Perl's Feersum library with a Socket.io-compatible middleware called PocketIO; the eventual Node.js rewrite used the ZappaJS framework and reportedly took about an afternoon to produce a roughly 80-line working server. To let the existing browser-only SocialCalc JavaScript engine run headlessly on the server for exporting spreadsheets to static HTML or other formats, EtherCalc used jsdom to simulate a browser DOM inside Node.js; profiling later showed that the RenderSheet routine was a major performance bottleneck, and replacing the full jsdom dependency with a small, purpose-built 20-line DOM stub in that hot path improved throughput roughly fourfold and HTML export performance by about twenty times. Tang also built and published webworker-threads, a cross-platform implementation of the Web Worker API for Node.js, so that each collaborative spreadsheet room could run its command execution in a separate background thread and take advantage of multi-core servers. Tang further stress-tested and refined the project during a Trend Micro hackathon in April 2012. A Redis-backed persistence layer was added so spreadsheet state could survive server restarts, and the public instance at ethercalc.net was launched for open beta use.

Architecturally, the classic version of EtherCalc runs as a Node.js (later LiveScript-compiled) server process that holds each open spreadsheet room in memory, synchronizes changes to connected clients over WebSockets using an event-based model (clients send locally executed commands and cursor movements to the server, which relays them to every other client in that room), and persists room state to Redis. It exposes a REST and JSON API documented in the repository's API.md and via an Apiary specification, supporting GET and PUT operations on individual cells, POST submission of raw SocialCalc commands, and export or import in multiple serializations including CSV, Excel XML, and the native SocialCalc format, so external systems and scripts can read or programmatically drive a spreadsheet without a browser. Self-hosting has historically been possible via npm install, via one-click deploy buttons to platform-as-a-service providers such as Heroku, DotCloud, and Nodejitsu, or on a self-managed server; the AOSA case-study chapter on the project describes production deployments ranging from small behind-the-firewall installs (roughly two CPU cores and 4 GB of RAM) up to a cloud-hosted multi-tenant deployment serving more than 35,000 companies. The project has continued to evolve well past its original Node.js and Redis design: the current GitHub repository has been substantially rewritten in TypeScript targeting Cloudflare's developer platform, using the Hono web framework, Cloudflare Workers, Durable Objects with SQLite for room state, and D1, KV, and R2 for storage, while still supporting fully self-hosted operation through Docker Compose or a standalone workerd runtime with no Cloudflare account required; a migration script (migrate-legacy.sh) is provided to move data from the older Redis-backed deployments (preserved under the git tag 0.20201228.1) into the new backend. Optional HMAC-based authentication (via an ETHERCALC_KEY environment variable), rate limiting, configurable room expiration for inactive spreadsheets, and a maximum request body size are available for anyone exposing an instance to the public internet, typically behind a reverse proxy for TLS termination.

EtherCalc's licensing reflects its layered history rather than a single company's standard terms: the original SocialCalc code contributed by Socialtext is covered by the Common Public Attribution License (CPAL) and the Artistic License 2.0, bundled third-party components such as jQuery and jszip.js are MIT-licensed, SheetJS-derived spreadsheet parsing components are Apache 2.0, icons adapted from LibreOffice are under the Mozilla Public License 2.0, and the newer TypeScript rewrite's own source is released into the public domain under CC0. In practice this means EtherCalc is free, open-source software that anyone can inspect, modify, self-host, and redistribute, with no proprietary tiers, subscriptions, or paid feature gates from the project itself.

EtherCalc's target users are developers who want to embed a lightweight, scriptable, and self-hostable spreadsheet component into another application (a Drupal 'sheetnode' plugin exists for this purpose), technically minded teams and educators who want frictionless real-time collaboration without requiring an account, sign-up, or a proprietary cloud provider, and privacy- or sovereignty-conscious organizations that prefer to run their own infrastructure rather than store data with Google or Microsoft. The most prominent public example of third-party adoption is Framacalc, an instance of EtherCalc lightly customized and operated by the French nonprofit Framasoft (founded 2004), which promotes free-software and privacy-respecting alternatives to mainstream cloud tools as part of its broader 'De-google-ify Internet' initiative; Framacalc has been highlighted in Framasoft's own blog posts as a flagship example of EtherCalc in production use outside its original creator's site. EtherCalc is also listed in resources such as the Free Software Directory, ISTE's education-technology resource listings, and self-hosting or 'homelab' community catalogs, reflecting its niche as a go-to open-source pick when someone wants Etherpad-style real-time collaboration applied specifically to spreadsheet data.

Relative to Google Sheets or Excel Online, EtherCalc trades away deep formula libraries, rich charting, add-on ecosystems, and enterprise access controls for simplicity, transparency, and full control: there is no account system by default, any spreadsheet's room URL is both its address and its access key unless an operator adds authentication, and the entire stack can be audited or self-hosted rather than trusted to a third-party cloud vendor. Compared to Etherpad, which pioneered this same room-URL, no-login, real-time collaborative model for plain text, EtherCalc is effectively its spreadsheet counterpart, sharing the same 'share a link and start co-editing' philosophy. One further notable fact about the project is biographical: EtherCalc's principal author, Audrey Tang, later became Taiwan's first Digital Minister, and the open-source, transparency-oriented ethos visible in EtherCalc's architecture and licensing choices is consistent with the civic-technology work Tang became known for afterward. Today the project remains actively maintained on GitHub under the audreyt/ethercalc repository, with an ongoing migration from its original Node.js and Redis architecture to a modern TypeScript and Cloudflare Workers implementation, while preserving the original goal that has defined it since 2011: making spreadsheet collaboration as immediate and frictionless as sharing a link.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: EtherCalc is a free, open-source web spreadsheet designed from the ground up for real-time collaboration. Instead of documents locked behind an account system, every EtherCalc spreadsheet lives at a shareable room URL, and anyone with that link can view or edit the sheet, with changes appearing live on every connected participant's screen.

The project descends from Dan Bricklin's WikiCalc and Socialtext's SocialCalc, and was rewritten into a Node.js application by Audrey Tang starting in 2011 as an experiment in real-time, multi-user spreadsheet synchronization. It is maintained on GitHub under audreyt/ethercalc and can be run as a hosted public service (ethercalc.net) or self-hosted on a company's own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core to EtherCalc is its room-based, no-signup collaboration model: a spreadsheet is created simply by visiting a URL, and editing rights are shared by sharing that link, with optional HMAC-based authentication for restricted deployments.

It exposes a REST and JSON API for programmatic reads and writes to individual cells or whole sheets, supports CSV and Excel XML import and export, and can be embedded into other systems, such as Drupal sites via a dedicated sheetnode plugin.

It is fully self-hostable via npm, Docker Compose, or a standalone server process, and the current codebase has been rewritten in TypeScript to also support deployment on Cloudflare Workers with Durable Objects for state, while keeping self-hosting available with no cloud account required.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: EtherCalc is open-source software distributed under a mix of open licenses (CPAL and Artistic License 2.0 for the original SocialCalc-derived code, MIT and Apache 2.0 for bundled libraries, and CC0 for the newer TypeScript rewrite), and there is no paid tier from the project itself.

Teams can use the free public instance at ethercalc.net, use a community-run instance such as Framasoft's Framacalc, or self-host their own copy at no license cost, only paying for whatever server or Cloudflare Workers resources they choose to run it on.

features.title: Real-time multi-user editing
features.description: Every cell change, formula edit, and cursor movement is broadcast over WebSockets to all participants in a spreadsheet room the instant it happens, with no manual save or refresh required.

features.title: No sign-up, link-based access
features.description: Spreadsheets are created and accessed via room URLs; anyone with the link can view or edit by default, making it trivial to share a working spreadsheet the same way Etherpad shares a document.

features.title: REST and JSON API
features.description: A documented API supports GET and PUT on individual cells, POST of raw SocialCalc commands, and full-sheet reads and writes, letting other applications or scripts drive a spreadsheet programmatically.

features.title: Multiple export and import formats
features.description: Sheets can be exported to or imported from CSV, Excel XML, and the native SocialCalc serialization, easing interchange with desktop spreadsheet tools.

features.title: Self-hostable deployment
features.description: EtherCalc can be installed via npm, run with Docker Compose, or deployed as a standalone server, giving teams full control over where their spreadsheet data lives.

features.title: Cloudflare Workers-native rewrite
features.description: The current codebase is a TypeScript rewrite built on Hono, Cloudflare Workers, and Durable Objects with SQLite-backed state, alongside D1, KV, and R2 for storage, while remaining self-hostable without a Cloudflare account.

features.title: Optional authentication and rate limiting
features.description: For internet-facing instances, operators can enable HMAC-based authentication via an ETHERCALC_KEY, configure request rate limits, and set expiration for inactive rooms.

features.title: CMS and third-party integration
features.description: A Drupal sheetnode plugin and the open API make it possible to embed live, editable EtherCalc spreadsheets inside other websites and platforms.

pros: Completely free and open source, with no account, subscription, or paid tier required to use it
Genuine real-time collaborative editing with no manual save step, similar in spirit to Etherpad but for spreadsheets
Fully self-hostable, giving organizations complete control over where spreadsheet data is stored
Documented REST and JSON API makes it easy to integrate with other tools and scripts
Lightweight compared to full office suites, so it starts and loads quickly even on modest hardware
cons: Formula library, charting, and formatting options are far more limited than Google Sheets or Excel Online
Default configuration grants anyone with a room URL edit access, so public deployments need extra care around authentication
No built-in enterprise access controls, audit logs, or granular permissions out of the box
The project is maintained largely as a personal open-source effort rather than by a dedicated commercial team, so support relies on community and documentation rather than a vendor help desk
Users self-hosting must handle their own infrastructure, backups, and migration between the legacy Redis-backed version and the newer Cloudflare Workers-based rewrite

faqs.question: What is EtherCalc?
faqs.answer: EtherCalc is a free, open-source, browser-based spreadsheet built for real-time collaboration, where multiple people can edit the same sheet simultaneously by sharing a room URL, with no sign-up required.

faqs.question: Who created EtherCalc?
faqs.answer: EtherCalc was created by Audrey Tang as a Node.js rewrite of Multi-user SocialCalc, starting around mid-2011. It builds on SocialCalc, developed by Socialtext, which itself grew out of Dan Bricklin's earlier WikiCalc project. Dan Bricklin is also known as the co-creator of VisiCalc, the first electronic spreadsheet.

faqs.question: Is EtherCalc free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. EtherCalc is open-source software released under a combination of open licenses, including CPAL, Artistic License 2.0, MIT, Apache 2.0, and CC0 for different parts of the codebase. There is no paid plan; you can use the public hosted instance, a community-run instance, or self-host it for free.

faqs.question: Can I self-host EtherCalc?
faqs.answer: Yes. EtherCalc can be installed via npm, run with Docker Compose, or deployed as a standalone server on your own infrastructure. The current codebase also supports deployment to Cloudflare Workers, while still working fully self-hosted with no Cloudflare account required.

faqs.question: Does EtherCalc have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes. EtherCalc provides a REST and JSON API for reading and writing individual cells or entire sheets, submitting spreadsheet commands, and exporting or importing data in CSV, Excel XML, or the native SocialCalc format.

faqs.question: How does EtherCalc compare to Google Sheets or Excel Online?
faqs.answer: EtherCalc offers a much smaller formula and formatting feature set than Google Sheets or Excel Online, but it requires no account, is fully open source, can be self-hosted for complete data control, and is lighter weight, making it a fit for developers, educators, and privacy-focused teams rather than a full replacement for enterprise office suites.

use_cases.title: Quick, no-signup collaborative data entry
use_cases.description: Teams share a single EtherCalc room URL to jointly fill in a data set, plan an event, or track a shared list in real time without anyone needing to create an account.
use_cases.audience: Small teams and ad hoc groups

use_cases.title: Embedding a live spreadsheet in another application
use_cases.description: Developers use EtherCalc's REST and JSON API, or the Drupal sheetnode plugin, to embed an editable, real-time spreadsheet directly inside another website, dashboard, or content management system.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted, privacy-respecting spreadsheet infrastructure
use_cases.description: Organizations that prefer not to store data with Google or Microsoft self-host EtherCalc via Docker or Cloudflare Workers, giving them full control over where spreadsheet data lives, similar to how Framasoft operates its own Framacalc instance.

Self-host on your own server via npm or Docker at no license cost
Deploy to Cloudflare Workers with Durable Objects, D1, KV, and R2
Full REST and JSON API access with no usage fees

enrichment.short_description: Etherpad is a free, open-source, web-based editor for real-time collaborative writing, letting multiple authors edit the same plain-text or rich-text document simultaneously with full attribution and version history.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Etherpad is a free, open-source real-time collaborative editor born from Google's 2009 open-sourcing of AppJet's product. See features, self-hosting, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Etherpad is a free, open-source, web-based editor purpose-built for real-time collaborative writing. Multiple people can open the same document, called a 'pad,' at a shared URL and type simultaneously, with every participant's keystrokes appearing on every other participant's screen within a fraction of a second. Each contributor is assigned a distinct highlight color so that authorship of any given passage is visually traceable, and the entire edit history of a pad is preserved and can be replayed character-by-character through a built-in 'timeslider' feature. Pads can be embedded directly into other web pages via an iframe, making Etherpad a common building block for wikis, community sites, and internal tools that need a lightweight, embeddable collaborative text surface rather than a full standalone application.

The project's origins trace back to AppJet Inc., a small startup founded by David Greenspan, Aaron Iba, and J.D. Zamfirescu, two of whom were former Google engineers. AppJet publicly launched Etherpad on November 19, 2008, as a hosted product at etherpad.com, and it quickly attracted attention as one of the first genuinely real-time, no-refresh-needed collaborative text editors available on the open web, at a time when Google Docs' collaborative features were still comparatively young. The product's technical core relied on operational transformation, an algorithm family used to reconcile concurrent edits from multiple users into a single consistent document without requiring locks or manual conflict resolution.

In December 2009, Google announced it was acquiring AppJet, primarily to bring its three co-founders onto the Google Wave team, itself an ambitious and short-lived collaborative-communication project. As part of that acquisition, Google initially planned to shut down the standalone Etherpad service and fold its technology and team into Wave. That plan triggered a fast and vocal backlash from the existing user community, journalists, open-source advocates, and organizations that had come to depend on Etherpad for real-time note-taking; the outcry played out across Hacker News, mailing lists, and tech press within days of the acquisition announcement. Google reversed course strikingly quickly: within about two weeks, on December 17, 2009, it released the full Etherpad source code under the Apache License 2.0, allowing the community to keep the software alive independently even as Google wound down the original hosted service (which it kept running for several more months, into 2010, to give users time to migrate or self-host).

Once the code was open, a self-organized group of users, developers, and former AppJet-adjacent contributors formed the Etherpad Foundation to steward the project going forward, a structure that persists as the software's governing body today. The original open-sourced codebase was written in Java and Scala and depended on the Jetty servlet container, which made it comparatively heavy to install and operate, especially for smaller organizations or hobbyist self-hosters who wanted a simple always-on pad server. Beginning in 2011, German developer Peter Martischka led a near-total rewrite of the software in server-side JavaScript running on Node.js, released under the name Etherpad Lite. This rewrite dramatically lowered the barrier to self-hosting, since it required only a Node.js runtime rather than a JVM stack, and it introduced a plugin architecture that let the community extend functionality without forking the core. Etherpad Lite reached rough feature parity with the original Java-based Etherpad by around 2012 to 2013, and by 2014 it had effectively superseded the legacy version; the Etherpad Foundation subsequently dropped the 'Lite' suffix, and the Node.js implementation became the canonical Etherpad that is still developed today under the ether/etherpad GitHub organization.

Technically, modern Etherpad runs as a Node.js server process (current releases target Node.js 24 and above) using a synchronization core historically known as 'Easysync,' an implementation of operational transformation tailored to plain and rich text editing, which allows it to handle many simultaneous editors on a single pad without the document falling out of sync. Persistence is handled through pluggable database back ends, including PostgreSQL and MySQL for production deployments and a lightweight file-based 'dirtyDB' store intended only for development or single-user testing. Every document lives at its own pad-based URL, which can be shared directly, protected with a password, made read-only, or embedded in an iframe on a third-party page for lightweight in-context collaboration. The client-side editor runs in the browser and communicates with the server over a persistent connection to push and receive incremental changes in near real time.

One of Etherpad's most distinctive technical traits is its plugin framework. Rather than trying to be a single monolithic editor, the core team keeps the base product minimal and lets a large community-maintained plugin ecosystem, numbering over a hundred published plugins, add capabilities such as Markdown support, code syntax highlighting, mathematical notation, inline comments and suggestions, drawing and image embedding, WebRTC-based video and audio chat alongside the text pad, table support, spellcheck, and authentication integrations for OAuth, LDAP, and OpenID. Administrators install plugins through a web admin panel or a command-line package manager, and because plugins are just Node.js packages, organizations can also write private, custom plugins for internal needs. Etherpad also exposes an HTTP API for programmatic pad creation, content retrieval, and user and group management, with community-maintained client libraries in PHP, Python, Ruby, Java, Perl, Objective-C, and JavaScript, which lets Etherpad function as an embeddable collaborative-editing backend inside other applications rather than only as a standalone site. Built-in export supports plain text, HTML, PDF, ODF, Microsoft Word, and LaTeX formats, and import generally accepts plain text and HTML, letting pads move in and out of other office tooling.

The entire codebase remains licensed under the permissive Apache License 2.0, which allows commercial use, modification, and redistribution, including by companies that build paid hosting or support services around the open-source core. There is no proprietary 'enterprise' fork; the same code that any hobbyist can install is what governments, universities, and large hosted providers run. Deployment options range from a one-line curl or PowerShell installer script, to manual setup with the pnpm package manager, to official Docker images published on both Docker Hub and the GitHub Container Registry, with ready-made docker-compose templates for quick production deployment. Because the software carries no telemetry, requires no forced updates, and stores all data in a database the operator fully controls, Etherpad is frequently chosen specifically for its 'server sovereignty' model: organizations that need strict control over where document data physically resides, or that cannot send sensitive text to a third-party cloud vendor, can run Etherpad entirely on infrastructure they own.

That positioning shapes who actually uses Etherpad today. Its most visible institutional adopter is the Wikimedia Foundation, which has long run its own Etherpad instance for coordinating Wikipedia and sister-project editing sessions, meetups, and technical planning documents. Beyond Wikimedia, Etherpad has seen sustained use among European Union public-sector bodies, universities, civic-tech and participatory-democracy projects such as citizen assemblies and public consultations, newsrooms doing collaborative live-blogging or story drafting, hackathons, and open-source projects that need a quick shared scratchpad without spinning up a heavier tool. Its natural audience is less the general office worker and more open-source communities, journalists, digital-rights and activist organizations, educators running workshops, developers who want an embeddable editor component, and privacy- or compliance-conscious organizations that prefer or require self-hosted software.

Within the broader landscape of collaborative editors, Etherpad is most often compared to Google Docs, HackMD (now known as HedgeDoc in its self-hosted community fork), and CryptPad. Against Google Docs, Etherpad trades away rich formatting depth, comments-as-suggestions workflows, and deep spreadsheet or presentation tooling in exchange for being lightweight, self-hostable, and free of any vendor lock-in or telemetry. Against HackMD and HedgeDoc, which are Markdown-first note tools oriented toward developer documentation, Etherpad is more of a general-purpose plain and rich-text pad without a strict Markdown authoring model, though Markdown support is available as a plugin. Against CryptPad, which emphasizes end-to-end, zero-knowledge encryption so that even the hosting server cannot read document contents, Etherpad does not encrypt data at rest by default; its privacy story instead rests on the operator controlling the server itself rather than on cryptographic guarantees against the server operator. Etherpad's differentiation, in short, is its combination of maturity (it is one of the oldest continuously maintained real-time collaborative editors on the web, predating Google Docs' own real-time co-editing by a couple of years in its original AppJet form), its extensible plugin architecture, and its unambiguous open-source, self-hostable licensing.

The project today is maintained by a small, largely volunteer team under the Etherpad Foundation, which has publicly stated it is looking for additional maintainers and contributors with Node.js or real-time-systems experience. Recent development has focused on modernizing the codebase, including a shift of a large portion of the code to TypeScript, updating minimum supported Node.js versions, refreshing the installation and Docker experience, and adding optional AI-assisted writing features implemented as plugins that connect to a model of the administrator's choosing rather than a bundled third-party AI service. The current stable release line is in the 3.x series as of mid-2026. Etherpad remains free to use, with no tiered commercial pricing from the core project itself; any cost associated with running it comes from the hosting infrastructure an organization chooses, not from a license fee.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Etherpad is a free, open-source, web-based editor that lets multiple people write and edit the same document at the same time, with every keystroke appearing live on everyone else's screen. Each collaborator's text is highlighted in a distinct color, making it easy to see who contributed what, and a built-in timeslider lets anyone scrub back through the full edit history of a pad.

The project traces back to AppJet Inc., a startup that launched the original Etherpad in November 2008. Google acquired AppJet in December 2009 mainly to bring its founders onto the Google Wave team, and initially planned to shut Etherpad down. A fast community backlash led Google to open-source the code under Apache License 2.0 just two weeks later, on December 17, 2009. The community-run Etherpad Foundation formed afterward to steward the project, and a 2011 Node.js rewrite, originally called Etherpad Lite, eventually became the canonical Etherpad still developed today.

Because it is fully open source and self-hostable, Etherpad is popular with organizations that want full control over where their document data lives, including the Wikimedia Foundation, universities, civic-tech projects, newsrooms, and privacy-conscious teams that prefer to avoid third-party cloud editors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Etherpad uses operational transformation, via its 'Easysync' synchronization engine, to merge concurrent edits from any number of simultaneous authors into one consistent document in near real time, without locking sections or requiring manual conflict resolution.

Documents live at simple, shareable pad-based URLs and can be embedded directly into other websites through an iframe, made read-only, or protected with a password, which makes Etherpad useful both as a standalone tool and as an embeddable collaborative-editing component inside other products.

A plugin framework, with well over a hundred community-built plugins, extends the lightweight core with features like Markdown authoring, code syntax highlighting, inline comments, tables, drawing, spellcheck, WebRTC video and audio chat alongside the text pad, and OAuth, LDAP, or OpenID authentication, while an HTTP API and client libraries in several languages let developers automate pad creation and manage content programmatically.

Built-in export supports plain text, HTML, PDF, ODF, Microsoft Word, and LaTeX, and the software can be self-hosted via Docker, a one-line installer script, or manual Node.js and pnpm setup, backed by PostgreSQL or MySQL for production use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Etherpad itself has no license fee: the entire codebase is released under the Apache License 2.0, which permits free use, modification, and even commercial redistribution by anyone, including companies that offer paid hosting or support around the open-source core.

There is a single 'plan' in the sense that every user runs the same software; any actual cost comes from self-hosting infrastructure, such as a small VPS or Docker host, that an organization chooses to run Etherpad on, not from Etherpad itself. There is no tiered commercial pricing, seat-based licensing, or paywalled feature set from the core project.

faqs.question: Is Etherpad free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Etherpad is fully open source under the Apache License 2.0, with no license fees. Any cost comes only from the hosting infrastructure an organization uses to run it, since there is no official paid tier from the core project.

faqs.question: Who originally created Etherpad?
faqs.answer: Etherpad was created by AppJet Inc., a startup founded by David Greenspan, Aaron Iba, and J.D. Zamfirescu, and launched publicly in November 2008. Google acquired AppJet in December 2009 and open-sourced the code that same month after community backlash to its planned shutdown.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Etherpad and Etherpad Lite?
faqs.answer: Etherpad Lite was a Node.js rewrite of the original Java and Scala-based Etherpad, started by developer Peter Martischka in 2011. It became feature-complete by around 2013 to 2014 and was then simply renamed Etherpad, becoming the canonical version that is still developed today.

faqs.question: Can Etherpad be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosting is Etherpad's primary deployment model. It can be installed via a one-line installer script, manual Node.js and pnpm setup, or official Docker images, and supports PostgreSQL or MySQL as production databases.

faqs.question: Does Etherpad support plugins and an API?
faqs.answer: Yes. Etherpad has an extensible plugin framework with over a hundred community plugins covering Markdown, comments, video chat, authentication, and more, plus an HTTP API with client libraries in several languages for programmatic pad management.

faqs.question: How does Etherpad compare to Google Docs?
faqs.answer: Etherpad offers lighter-weight, embeddable, real-time text collaboration without the rich formatting, spreadsheet, or presentation features of Google Docs. Its main advantage is that it is free, open source, and self-hostable, giving organizations full control over where document data is stored rather than relying on Google's cloud.

use_cases.description: Because pads can be embedded via iframe and managed through an HTTP API, developers use Etherpad as a lightweight collaborative-editing component inside wikis, internal tools, and community platforms.

use_cases.description: Newsrooms, educators, and civic-tech or activist organizations use Etherpad for real-time collaborative drafting during live events, workshops, hackathons, and public consultations, where quick setup and no vendor lock-in matter.

enrichment.seo_title: evcc Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: evcc is an open-source EV charging controller for solar-surplus and dynamic-tariff charging. See features, sponsor-token pricing, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
evcc is an open-source EV charge controller and home energy management system written in Go that automates when and how fast an electric vehicle charges based on available solar surplus, dynamic electricity tariffs, or a combination of both. Rather than being a commercial SaaS product built by a company, evcc is a community-maintained software project hosted on GitHub under the evcc-io organization, distributed under the permissive MIT license, and designed to be self-hosted on hardware the user already owns, such as a Raspberry Pi, a Docker host, a NAS, an x86 Linux machine, or macOS, with an official Home Assistant add-on also available. The project's stated philosophy is local-first energy management, meaning the core charging-control logic runs entirely on the user's own device without any mandatory cloud dependency, and the project explicitly markets that a user's data stays with them rather than being routed through a vendor's servers.

The evcc GitHub repository shows a creation date of December 6, 2019, and public reporting on the project's history indicates the first substantive commits landed in early 2020 from the primary author, Andreas Linde, who goes by the GitHub handle andig. The stated motivation for starting evcc was dissatisfaction with the architecture of openWB, an earlier and still-popular German open-source EV charging controller that relies heavily on shell scripting and global state; Linde wanted a more structured, strongly typed alternative and built evcc in Go with a proper plugin abstraction layer instead. Today the project is led principally by two maintainers, Andreas Linde and Michael Geers, supported by a broader base of community contributors who submit device drivers, bug fixes, and translations. As of mid-2026 the evcc-io/evcc repository on GitHub has accumulated roughly 6,960 stars and 1,409 forks, with around 129 open issues and 66 repository subscribers, and the evcc-io GitHub organization hosts roughly two dozen related repositories, including a companion mobile app, an optimizer service for EV and battery charging, and Home Assistant integration tooling.

Technically, evcc is built as a single Go binary that exposes a local web UI and a REST and WebSocket API. The frontend configuration and dashboard interface is implemented in Vue and TypeScript, which together with Go make up the overwhelming majority of the codebase. The core of the product is a plugin and driver architecture that lets it talk to almost any charger, smart switch, heat pump, electric heater, meter, solar or battery inverter, or vehicle through a common set of protocols: Modbus, HTTP, MQTT, JavaScript, WebSocket, native Go drivers, and shell script hooks. This protocol-agnostic design is what allows evcc to claim support for a very large and heterogeneous device ecosystem; project documentation and community sources describe support for well over 240 manufacturers and more than 620 specific hardware products spanning wallbox chargers (go-eCharger, KEBA, Easee, Wallbox, ABB, Tesla Wall Connector, and many others), solar and battery inverters (Fronius, SMA, Huawei, Tesla, and others), smart meters (ABB, Schneider Electric, Siemens, and others), and dozens of vehicle makes including Tesla, BMW, Audi, Volkswagen, Hyundai, Kia, Mercedes-Benz, and Porsche, either through direct vehicle APIs or via bridge services such as TeslaMate, Tronity, and OVMS. evcc also implements standardized industry protocols including OCPP, EEBus, SunSpec, and SEMP, which lets it interoperate with newer smart-grid-ready hardware without a bespoke integration for every device.

The core charging behaviors evcc automates are solar-surplus charging, where the software continuously adjusts EV charging current to match the excess power a home's solar array is generating, minimizing grid draw and maximizing self-consumption, and tariff-based charging, where it can shift charging into the cheapest or lowest-carbon hours by reading real-time or day-ahead spot electricity prices. On top of these two modes, users can define charging plans that specify a target charge level or a full battery state paired with a desired departure or ready-by time, and evcc will backward-plan the cheapest or greenest charging schedule that still meets that deadline. The software also coordinates a home battery and a heat pump alongside EV charging so that the different loads and storage devices in a household are optimized together rather than independently, and it supports load management across multiple chargers so that a fixed electrical circuit is not overloaded when more than one vehicle charges at once. In markets with regulated grid operator demand-response requirements, notably Germany's Paragraph 14a rules for controllable consumption devices, evcc has added specific support for complying with those grid-operator throttling signals. Status and event notifications can be routed through Telegram, Pushover, and other channels, and charging history can be logged to InfluxDB and visualized in Grafana for users who want deeper analytics than the built-in dashboard provides.

evcc's business model reflects its identity as an open-source project rather than a company. The core software and the vast majority of device drivers are free to use under the MIT license with no functional restrictions. To fund ongoing development, the project operates a voluntary sponsorship program: supporters can back the project through GitHub Sponsors at four US dollars per month, or purchase a one-time lifetime sponsor token for roughly one hundred fifty euros through the project's own Creem.io-based storefront at sponsor.evcc.io, with volume-discounted token bundles available for electricians and installers who deploy evcc commercially for clients. The practical effect of a sponsor token is that it unlocks a subset of premium device drivers and features, such as certain wallbox integrations and a telemetry feature, that vendors require compensation to support; everything else in the product remains free regardless of sponsorship status. Contributors who make significant code contributions, and the first person to complete a full UI translation into a new language, receive a free sponsor token as a reward, and the interface itself is translated collaboratively through a hosted Weblate instance, with German, English, Italian, and Lithuanian confirmed as available UI languages as of this research, and additional community translations added on an ongoing basis.

evcc's primary audience is homeowners in Germany and elsewhere in Europe who have installed rooftop solar, a home battery, and an EV charger and want to automatically prioritize self-generated solar power for vehicle charging rather than manually monitoring production and toggling the charger by hand. A secondary and closely related audience is the broader DIY and smart-home enthusiast community, since running evcc typically requires a degree of technical comfort with self-hosting software, editing YAML configuration files or using the built-in configuration UI, and networking devices like smart meters and wallboxes onto a home LAN. Because evcc is not tied to any single hardware vendor, it also appeals to installers and electricians who want a single unified control layer that works across mixed-brand installations rather than being locked into one manufacturer's proprietary app.

The most direct point of comparison in the market is openWB, the older German open-source EV charging project that evcc's creator explicitly built evcc to improve upon; openWB relies more heavily on shell scripting and a monolithic architecture, while evcc offers a more modular, typed, and API-driven design with a modern Vue-based web interface. Beyond openWB, evcc also competes indirectly with the closed, single-vendor charging apps bundled with individual wallbox or EV manufacturers, none of which offer evcc's cross-brand device compatibility or fully local operation. Within the Home Assistant ecosystem, evcc is also frequently deployed alongside or as an alternative to Home-Assistant-native energy dashboards, and the project ships an official Home Assistant add-on to make that integration straightforward. Overall, evcc has established itself as one of the most widely adopted open-source EV smart-charging controllers in the German- and English-speaking DIY solar and EV community, distinguished by its breadth of device support, its local-first no-cloud-required architecture, its permissive MIT licensing, and a lightweight, community-funded sponsorship model that keeps the core product free while still supporting continued maintenance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: evcc is a free, open-source EV charge controller and home energy management system written in Go. It runs locally on hardware the user already owns, such as a Raspberry Pi, a Docker host, a NAS, or an x86 Linux or macOS machine, and it also ships as an official Home Assistant add-on.

Rather than being built by a company, evcc is maintained as a community project on GitHub under the evcc-io organization. The GitHub repository was created on December 6, 2019, with the project's founder, Andreas Linde (GitHub handle andig), citing frustration with the shell-script-heavy architecture of the older German open-source project openWB as the reason he started building evcc as a more structured, plugin-based alternative in Go.

The project is licensed under the permissive MIT license and, as of this research, has accumulated roughly 6,960 stars and 1,409 forks on GitHub, with active maintenance led by Andreas Linde and Michael Geers alongside a broader community of contributors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: evcc's central feature is solar-surplus charging, where the software continuously adjusts EV charging current so that a vehicle draws power roughly in step with the excess electricity a home's solar array is generating, minimizing pulls from the grid. Alongside that, it supports dynamic-tariff charging that shifts charging into the cheapest or lowest-carbon hours available on a spot-price electricity contract, and charging plans where a user sets a target charge level and a ready-by time and lets evcc backward-plan the schedule.

Through a protocol-agnostic plugin architecture built on Modbus, HTTP, MQTT, JavaScript, WebSocket, native Go drivers, and shell scripts, evcc integrates with a very large and mixed hardware ecosystem, reportedly covering more than 240 manufacturers and over 620 specific products across chargers, solar and battery inverters, smart meters, heat pumps, and vehicles. It also implements standardized protocols such as OCPP, EEBus, SunSpec, and SEMP for newer smart-grid-ready devices, and in Germany it supports the regulatory Paragraph 14a grid-operator throttling requirements for controllable consumption devices.

Beyond charging control, evcc coordinates home batteries and heat pumps alongside EV charging, manages load balancing across multiple chargers on one circuit, sends notifications through channels like Telegram and Pushover, offers companion iOS and Android apps for monitoring, and can log detailed charging history to InfluxDB for visualization in Grafana.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: evcc's core software and the large majority of its device drivers are completely free under the MIT license with no functional limitations. The project sustains itself through a voluntary sponsorship model rather than a traditional subscription.

Supporters can sponsor the project through GitHub Sponsors at four US dollars per month, or buy a one-time lifetime sponsor token for approximately one hundred fifty euros through the project's own storefront at sponsor.evcc.io, with volume-discounted token bundles available for electricians and installers deploying evcc for clients. A sponsor token unlocks a small subset of premium device drivers and a telemetry feature that require compensation to vendors; everything else in evcc remains fully free regardless of sponsorship status. Significant code contributors and the first translator to complete a new UI language also receive a free sponsor token.

faqs.answer: Yes. evcc's core software is free and open source under the MIT license. A small subset of premium device drivers and a telemetry feature require a paid or earned sponsor token, but the vast majority of functionality has no cost.

faqs.question: What hardware can evcc run on?
faqs.answer: evcc runs on a Raspberry Pi, inside Docker on a NAS or server, on x86 Linux, on macOS, or as an official Home Assistant add-on. It is designed to run locally rather than depend on a vendor cloud service.

faqs.question: Who maintains evcc?
faqs.answer: evcc is a community-driven open-source project on GitHub under the evcc-io organization, founded by Andreas Linde and currently led by maintainers Andreas Linde and Michael Geers along with community contributors, funded through voluntary GitHub Sponsors and sponsor token purchases.

faqs.question: Which EV chargers and vehicles does evcc support?
faqs.answer: evcc reports support for more than 240 hardware manufacturers and over 620 specific products, including chargers such as go-eCharger, KEBA, Easee, and Wallbox, and vehicles from Tesla, BMW, Audi, Volkswagen, Hyundai, Kia, and other major automakers.

faqs.question: How is evcc different from openWB?

enrichment.short_description: EventMobi is a Toronto-based event management platform offering event apps, registration, onsite check-in and badge printing, and virtual and hybrid streaming tools for conferences and corporate events.

enrichment.seo_title: EventMobi Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: EventMobi review: event apps, registration, badge printing, and hybrid streaming. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
EventMobi is an end-to-end event management platform built for corporate event planners, associations, and conference organizers who run in-person, virtual, and hybrid events. The product suite covers the full lifecycle of an event: an event website builder for pre-event marketing and registration pages, an online registration and ticketing engine with no added ticketing fees, a native and web-based event app that attendees use to browse agendas, view speaker bios, message other attendees, and receive push notifications, onsite tools for badge printing and check-in, live polling and audience Q and A for sessions, networking and matchmaking features that suggest relevant connections to attendees, lead capture tools for exhibitors and sponsors, and a virtual and hybrid event platform with live streaming, on-demand video, and virtual expo booths. Organizers manage all of this from a single backend dashboard, importing sessions, speakers, sponsors, and attendee lists, then pushing real-time updates to attendees as agendas change.

The company traces its origins to 2009, when founder Bob Vaez, then an engineer attending business school, built a prototype mobile guide as a side project after growing frustrated with the bulky printed show guides handed out at conferences. Vaez and his brother Bijan Vaez formally launched the company in Toronto in 2010, initially focused on a single product: a mobile event guide app that let attendees view session schedules on their phones instead of paper programs and participate in live polls during sessions. The company grew without external venture capital, remaining privately held and bootstrapped by reinvesting revenue back into product development. In 2013, EventMobi acquired Understoodit, a Toronto-based live polling and audience response tool, folding that functionality directly into its own platform. By 2016 the company had grown past 80 employees and opened a European office in Berlin to serve an expanding international client base. When the COVID-19 pandemic forced the events industry to shift online in 2020, EventMobi launched a Virtual Event Space product to let organizers run fully remote conferences with streaming sessions, virtual booths, and online networking, following the same trajectory as competitors like Hopin and Bizzabo during that period. As in-person events returned, EventMobi repositioned around hybrid delivery rather than abandoning the virtual tooling it had built. In August 2023, EventMobi acquired Run The World, a Silicon Valley-based virtual events platform backed by Andreessen Horowitz that had hosted more than 50,000 events, a deal covered by TechCrunch that expanded EventMobi's virtual event capabilities and customer base. The company today reports serving more than 30,000 event planners across over 92 countries, with more than 16.5 million attendees having passed through events run on its platform.

Technically, EventMobi operates as a cloud-hosted SaaS platform accessible through a web-based organizer dashboard, with native iOS and Android apps for attendees and dedicated apps that can be white-labeled for individual events or built as a persistent app for organizations running many events per year. The platform is built around modular products, meaning organizers pick and pay for only the components they need for a given event, such as Registration, Badge Printing, Check-In, Event App, Lead Capture, and Virtual Event streaming, and these modules share a common attendee and session data layer so that updates in one area, like a speaker cancellation, propagate to the app, website, and onsite badge systems automatically. EventMobi provides a free API and a native Zapier integration for connecting to external systems such as CRMs, marketing automation tools, and payment processors, and the company states it maintains SOC compliance and GDPR certification for handling attendee data across international events.

Pricing follows two primary models rather than flat per-seat SaaS tiers, reflecting the project-based nature of event planning. Per-event pricing starts at $3,000 and scales upward based on attendee count and which product modules are selected; this suits organizations running a single conference or an infrequent flagship event. Per-year pricing starts at $8,900 annually and includes unlimited events on the platform, a dedicated Event Success Manager, the lowest effective per-attendee cost, and the ability to carry forward unused attendee licenses into future periods, making it the better value for associations or corporations running two or more events a year. On top of these base plans, EventMobi charges for add-ons: pre-printed badges start at roughly $3.99 per badge including shipping in supported countries, onsite badge-printing hardware rental packages typically start around $1,000 for a multi-day rental, and streaming and video storage for virtual or hybrid sessions is sold in usage-based packs starting at $500 per event. Standard third-party integrations start at $1,000 per integration, with custom integration work quoted separately. EventMobi does not charge registration or ticketing fees beyond standard payment-processor fees, and it offers discounts for nonprofits, associations, and multi-year contracts. Organizations with more than 10,000 attendees or unusual requirements are directed to custom enterprise pricing. A free trial is available, typically unlocked after a personalized sales demo rather than as a self-serve signup.

EventMobi's target audience centers on corporate event planners, marketing and comms teams running internal and external events, associations and membership organizations running annual conferences, and nonprofits running fundraising or advocacy events. Named customers referenced in company materials and press include Visa, Ben and Jerry's, Yale University, Porsche, Shoppers Drug Mart, TD Bank, Forbes, Meta, Amazon, Harvard University, the American Medical Society for Sports Medicine, the World Health Organization, and Humane Canada, spanning corporate, academic, healthcare, and nonprofit sectors.

In the competitive landscape, EventMobi sits alongside Cvent, Whova, Bizzabo, Eventbrite, Swapcard, and the former Hopin (now largely absorbed into RingCentral Events) as an event technology vendor. Compared to Cvent, which is a much larger, enterprise-oriented suite with deeper venue-sourcing and strategic meetings management tools, EventMobi is generally positioned as a more accessible, mid-market platform with a simpler modular pricing structure and a stronger self-reported emphasis on customer support through dedicated Event Success Managers. Compared to Whova and Eventbrite, which lean toward lower-cost, self-serve event apps and ticketing for smaller events, EventMobi differentiates on its onsite badge printing and check-in hardware, its bundled virtual and hybrid streaming stack following the Run The World acquisition, and its focus on white-labeled, branded event apps for corporate and association clients rather than a shared multi-event marketplace app. Reviewers on G2 and Capterra consistently highlight EventMobi's ease of use for both organizers and attendees, the ability to centralize session, speaker, sponsor, and attendee data in one place, real-time agenda updates pushed via notifications, and responsive customer support as core strengths. Commonly cited drawbacks include the per-event starting price feeling high for very small or infrequent events, additional charges for streaming and video storage add-ons, some tedium in manually reordering back-to-back sessions in the agenda builder, and requests from some users for deeper bilingual and localization customization within the app interface. Overall, EventMobi occupies a position as a bootstrapped, profitable, mid-sized Canadian event technology company that has expanded from a single mobile conference guide into a full event management suite spanning registration, onsite operations, and virtual and hybrid delivery, competing on service quality and modular pricing rather than trying to match the enterprise scale of Cvent.

EventMobi's leadership team beyond founder and CEO Bob Vaez includes a CFO, a VP of Engineering and Product, a VP of Strategic Partnerships, a COO, and a VP of Marketing, reflecting a company that has scaled from a two-person side project into a structured, multi-department organization while staying privately held. The company describes itself as the trusted event technology provider for member-based organizations and corporations, and lists clients such as the American Medical Society for Sports Medicine, the World Health Organization, and Humane Canada alongside larger corporate accounts, underscoring a customer base that spans healthcare associations, global health bodies, nonprofits, and Fortune-level brands. Its self-reported claim of being used across more than 92 countries and by over 30,000 event planners points to a platform that, while smaller in headcount than giants like Cvent, has achieved broad international distribution, aided by its early European expansion through the Berlin office opened in 2016. Product-wise, EventMobi's trajectory mirrors the broader event technology market: it started as a single-purpose mobile guide app in the pre-smartphone-saturation era of 2009 to 2010, broadened into a full registration and engagement suite through the 2013 Understoodit acquisition and subsequent organic feature additions across the mid-2010s, pivoted hard toward virtual delivery during the pandemic-driven shutdown of in-person events in 2020, and then consolidated its position in hybrid event delivery through the 2023 acquisition of Run The World, absorbing that company's virtual event technology and portfolio of hosted events rather than building equivalent streaming infrastructure from scratch. This acquisition-and-build pattern, funded entirely from operating revenue rather than venture capital rounds, distinguishes EventMobi from venture-backed rivals such as the former Hopin, which raised over a billion dollars before contracting sharply once in-person events resumed, and positions EventMobi as a comparatively conservative, profitability-oriented operator within the event technology sector.
