enrichment.short_description: Evergreen is a free, open-source integrated library system (ILS) that public and academic libraries use to manage cataloging, circulation, patron accounts, and consortial resource sharing across large multi-branch networks.
enrichment.seo_title: Evergreen ILS Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Evergreen ILS review: a free, open-source library system for cataloging, circulation and consortial sharing. See features, architecture, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Evergreen, often referred to as Evergreen ILS, is an open-source integrated library system used by public and academic libraries to manage the core operations of running a library: cataloging materials, checking items in and out, tracking patron accounts and fines, placing and fulfilling holds, and coordinating acquisitions and serials. What sets Evergreen apart from many single-branch library systems is that it was built from the outset to support large numbers of independently governed libraries sharing one unified catalog, one shared pool of holdings, and one circulation and holds network, which makes it a common choice for statewide and regional library consortia rather than just standalone institutions.

The project traces its origin to a decision made by the Georgia Public Library Service (GPLS) in 2004. GPLS ran the Public Information Network for Electronic Services (PINES), a resource-sharing consortium that at the time served 252 public libraries across Georgia on the proprietary SirsiDynix Unicorn system. When Georgia state librarian Lamar Veatch announced that PINES would not renew its contract with Sirsi, GPLS chose to build its own automation system in-house rather than switch to another commercial vendor, reasoning that a custom system could better fit the consortium's scale and cost requirements. Development began in June 2004, and after more than two years of work the new system, named Evergreen, went live for the entire PINES consortium on September 5, 2006. The cutover was executed over Labor Day weekend 2006: the old Unicorn system was taken offline that Friday evening, and by the following Tuesday morning all patron records, item records and transaction histories for the 252-library consortium were running on Evergreen, on server hardware that reportedly cost roughly a quarter of what the previous proprietary system required. In 2007 the Andrew W. Mellon Foundation recognized the project with its Mellon Award for Technology Collaboration.

Evergreen's governance and support model evolved substantially after that initial launch. In 2007 members of the original development team formed Equinox Software, a commercial company offering paid hosting, migration, training and support services built around Evergreen; Equinox Software was later succeeded by the Equinox Open Library Initiative, a non-profit that continues to provide commercial hosting and support for libraries that want a managed deployment rather than self-hosting the open-source code themselves. On the community side, the Evergreen project joined the Software Freedom Conservancy as a fiscally sponsored project in 2012 and, as part of that relationship, formed an Evergreen Oversight Board made up of nine elected members drawn from service organizations, consortial staff and participating libraries, which serves as the top-level decision-making body for the software project. In 2019 the community elected an Evergreen Project board as part of a transition toward becoming a stand-alone non-profit organization. The community also runs an annual Evergreen Software Conference where developers, librarians and system administrators from participating consortia gather to discuss the roadmap, share implementation experience and coordinate development priorities.

Technically, Evergreen is built around a middleware layer called OpenSRF (the Open Scalable Request Framework, pronounced open surf), a message-based, decentralized service architecture that lets individual Evergreen services be distributed and scaled across many servers rather than running as a single monolithic application. This design was chosen specifically to let one Evergreen installation support very large numbers of simultaneous users and transactions, which was essential for a system serving hundreds of libraries at once. The underlying data store is PostgreSQL, and the majority of the business logic is written in Perl, with performance-sensitive sections implemented in C for speed. The patron-facing public catalog (OPAC) is rendered using Template Toolkit and JavaScript, while the staff-facing client, originally a XUL-based desktop application, has since transitioned to a browser-based interface built with Angular, reflecting the broader industry shift away from installed desktop clients toward web applications. On the standards side, Evergreen supports SIP2 for integrating with third-party self-checkout and self-service kiosks, Z39.50 and SRU for copy cataloging and search/retrieval against external bibliographic databases, and MARC21 as its core bibliographic and authority record format, alongside MODS metadata support.

Evergreen is licensed under the GNU General Public License (GPL-2.0-or-later), and its complete source code is publicly maintained on GitHub under the evergreen-library-system organization. Because the GPL guarantees the right to run, study, modify and redistribute the software, a library or consortium can legally download Evergreen and self-host it on its own PostgreSQL and Linux infrastructure at no licensing cost, though doing so requires in-house systems expertise; alternatively, many libraries contract with Equinox Open Library Initiative or similar vendors for managed hosting and support in exchange for a service fee, which is a common pattern in open-source software generally.

The primary audience for Evergreen is public library systems, academic libraries, and above all multi-branch and multi-jurisdiction library consortia that need a single shared catalog and circulation pool spanning many independently governed member libraries. Beyond the founding PINES consortium, which has grown from its original 252 libraries to more than 270, notable adopters over the years have included SITKA in British Columbia, Canada (roughly 92 multitype libraries and over 150 branches), NC Cardinal in North Carolina (216 libraries), CW MARS in Massachusetts (155 libraries), Evergreen Indiana (129 libraries), and Missouri Evergreen, part of the MOBIUS Consortium. Community reporting has put total worldwide Evergreen deployment at roughly 1,800 to 2,000 libraries across these and other consortia. The competitive landscape is not static, however: South Carolina's SC LENDS consortium, an earlier well-known Evergreen adopter, announced in 2023 that it was moving to SirsiDynix's Symphony platform, illustrating that consortia do periodically re-evaluate and switch ILS platforms as their needs and vendor relationships change.

Within the open-source ILS space, Evergreen's closest peer is Koha, another mature, actively developed open-source integrated library system. The two are frequently compared: Koha typically runs on MySQL and is generally regarded as somewhat easier to install, configure and maintain for a single library or a small group of libraries, with a larger overall global installed base, while Evergreen's PostgreSQL and OpenSRF foundation was purpose-built for very large, complex consortial resource-sharing arrangements and tends to be the stronger fit when many independently governed libraries need to operate as one shared system at scale. On the proprietary side, Evergreen and Koha both compete with commercial, vendor-hosted systems such as SirsiDynix Symphony and Ex Libris Alma, which bundle software, hosting and vendor support into a single subscription price; the trade-off libraries weigh is the zero licensing cost and full source-code control of Evergreen against the turnkey vendor support and integrated services that come standard with a commercial ILS contract.

Two decades after its original launch, Evergreen remains an actively maintained, community-governed open-source project rather than a commercial product with a single company behind it. Its evolution from a Georgia state library initiative into a globally deployed platform, its shift from a desktop XUL staff client to a modern web-based one, and its ongoing governance through an elected oversight board and annual community conference all reflect a project that has matured into a recognized, if still primarily public-sector, alternative to commercial library automation systems, particularly for large resource-sharing library consortia.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Evergreen is a free, open-source integrated library system (ILS) originally developed by the Georgia Public Library Service to run the PINES resource-sharing consortium, and it has since been adopted by public and academic library consortia around the world.

It handles the full range of core library operations, including cataloging (MARC21 bibliographic and authority records), circulation and holds, patron account management, acquisitions and serials, and a public search catalog, with an architecture designed to scale across large numbers of independently governed libraries sharing one system.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Evergreen is built on OpenSRF (Open Scalable Request Framework), a message-based service architecture backed by a PostgreSQL database, which lets a single installation scale to support hundreds of libraries and simultaneous users.

It supports standard library protocols including SIP2 for self-checkout hardware, Z39.50 and SRU for external cataloging and search, and MARC21 for bibliographic and authority records, alongside a web-based public catalog and staff client.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Evergreen itself is free: it is released under the GNU General Public License (GPL-2.0-or-later) and can be downloaded, self-hosted, and modified at no licensing cost.

Libraries that prefer not to self-host commonly contract with a support vendor such as Equinox Open Library Initiative for managed hosting, migration, and technical support, which is billed separately from the software itself since Evergreen has no vendor licensing fee.

features.title: Circulation and self-service
features.description: Checkout, renewals, holds, and fine management, with SIP2 protocol support for integrating third-party self-checkout kiosks and RFID hardware.

features.title: Browser-based staff client

features.title: Built-in reporting engine

features.title: OpenSRF service architecture
features.description: A stateless, decentralized middleware layer (Open Scalable Request Framework) that distributes Evergreen services across multiple servers, enabling large-scale horizontal deployment.

faqs.question: Is Evergreen ILS free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Evergreen is released under the GNU General Public License (GPL-2.0-or-later), so it can be downloaded, self-hosted, and modified at no licensing cost. Libraries that want managed hosting and support typically pay a separate vendor, such as Equinox Open Library Initiative, for that service.

faqs.question: Who governs and maintains the Evergreen project?
faqs.answer: Evergreen is community-governed through an elected Evergreen Oversight Board made up of representatives from participating libraries, consortia, and service organizations. The project has been fiscally sponsored by the Software Freedom Conservancy since 2012 and has been transitioning toward operating as a stand-alone non-profit since 2019.

faqs.question: What database and technology does Evergreen run on?
faqs.answer: Evergreen uses PostgreSQL as its database, with business logic written primarily in Perl and performance-critical sections in C. It runs on OpenSRF, a message-based service middleware that lets Evergreen services scale across multiple servers.

faqs.question: Is Evergreen designed for library consortia?
faqs.answer: Yes. It was originally built for the PINES consortium, a network of 252 Georgia public libraries sharing one catalog, and remains especially well-suited to statewide and regional consortia where many independently governed libraries need to share holdings and circulation.

faqs.question: What library standards does Evergreen support?
faqs.answer: Evergreen supports MARC21 for bibliographic and authority cataloging, SIP2 for self-checkout and self-service hardware integration, and Z39.50 and SRU for external search and copy cataloging.

faqs.question: How does Evergreen compare to Koha?
faqs.answer: Both are mature open-source integrated library systems. Koha generally runs on MySQL and is often considered easier to set up for a single library or small group, while Evergreen's PostgreSQL and OpenSRF architecture was purpose-built for large, complex multi-library consortial resource sharing at scale.

enrichment.seo_title: Everhour Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Everhour review 2026: time tracking, budgeting, and invoicing for teams with deep Asana, Trello, and Jira integrations. See pricing, features, pros, cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Everhour is a time tracking and project management platform built specifically to embed itself inside the project management tools that teams already use, rather than asking them to switch to a standalone timer app. Instead of positioning itself as a destination product, Everhour layers time tracking, budgeting, invoicing, resource planning, and reporting directly on top of tools like Asana, Trello, Basecamp, Jira, GitHub, ClickUp, Monday, Linear, Notion, and Wrike, using native integrations that inject a small timer widget and time-tracking controls into the task cards of those third-party apps. This 'integrate rather than replace' philosophy is the core technical and go-to-market differentiator that separates Everhour from more generalist competitors such as Toggl Track, Harvest, Clockify, TimeCamp, and Hubstaff.

Everhour was founded in 2015 by Mike Kulakov, Yury Tolochko, and Sergei Staroverov, a trio who had previously run a small IT outsourcing and web development company called Weavora. According to the company's own account of its history, the founders built Everhour internally because the time tracking tools available to them at the time were either too complicated for a small team or too limited in functionality to handle budgeting and client billing for an agency-style business. Everhour signed its first paying customer in September 2015 and, notably, grew to roughly one million dollars in annual recurring revenue within about three years without raising any venture capital. The company has remained self-funded and profitable throughout its existence, a detail it highlights prominently as part of its brand identity: an independent, bootstrapped alternative to venture-backed time tracking competitors. Public financial reporting from third-party sources places Everhour's revenue at roughly five million dollars with an estimated customer base of around 3,400 companies as of the mid-2020s, though these figures come from third-party estimates rather than official company disclosures and should be treated as approximate. The company is headquartered in Minsk, Belarus, with some listings also referencing Limassol, Cyprus, likely reflecting an operating or holding entity structure common among Eastern European-founded SaaS companies; Everhour itself does not prominently publish an official headquarters address on its marketing site. The team is small, generally estimated in the range of roughly fifteen to fifty people, consistent with a lean, profitable, founder-led SaaS business rather than a venture-scaled organization.

Technically, Everhour operates through a combination of a web application, a browser extension (available for Chrome and other Chromium-based browsers), and native integrations that inject Everhour's timer and reporting UI directly into the interface of the connected project management tool. When a user installs the Everhour browser extension and connects their Asana, Trello, Basecamp, GitHub, or Jira account, Everhour adds a small time-tracking widget to each task or issue card inside that third-party tool, allowing users to start and stop timers, log estimates, and view running totals without leaving their existing workflow. Time data recorded in this way syncs back to the Everhour web dashboard, where it can be aggregated into budgets, utilization reports, and client-ready invoices. On the finance side, Everhour supports project and task-level budgets defined either by hours or by monetary amount, with configurable notification thresholds (commonly cited at 75 percent, 90 percent, and 100 percent of budget consumption) and the ability to automatically stop timers once a budget cap is reached, which is particularly useful for agencies billing clients on fixed-fee or capped-hours contracts. Invoicing functionality lets teams generate client invoices directly from tracked billable hours and export that data to accounting platforms such as QuickBooks and Xero, closing the loop between time tracking and getting paid. Resource planning and scheduling features give managers a visual, calendar-style view of team capacity and availability across multiple clients and projects, helping agencies and consultancies avoid overbooking staff. Expense tracking allows teams to log non-time costs against a project budget so that total project profitability, not just billable hours, can be assessed. Reporting tools support customizable, schedulable reports and cross-project visibility, letting operations and finance stakeholders monitor utilization, billability, and budget health across an entire portfolio of client work.

Everhour's pricing follows a straightforward two-tier freemium structure with a custom enterprise tier layered on top. The Free plan supports up to five users at no cost and includes core time tracking, project and task tracking, reporting, and data export, but does not include the native third-party integrations that are central to Everhour's value proposition, meaning most real-world users of connected tools like Asana or Jira will need to upgrade. The paid Team plan is priced at 8.50 dollars per user per month when billed annually, or 10 dollars per user per month when billed monthly, with a minimum commitment of 5 seats (so the smallest paid team pays roughly 42.50 dollars per month billed annually). The Team plan unlocks the full feature set: unlimited native integrations, budgeting and budget alerts, invoicing, expense tracking, resource planning, single sign-on, and advanced permission controls, with the company explicitly marketing a 'no feature gating' policy across tiers of the paid plan rather than splitting functionality into good/better/best buckets. For organizations with more than 50 seats, Everhour offers a Custom plan with volume discounts, a dedicated account manager, and priority support, available on request. All paid plans include a 14-day free trial that does not require a credit card. Everhour bills exclusively in US dollars via credit or debit card, and the company differentiates between 'seats' (a baseline billing commitment) and 'users' (people actually assigned to the account), meaning removing a user does not automatically reduce the seat count a team is billed for.

Everhour's target audience centers on small and mid-sized teams that need to track billable time against client work: digital and marketing agencies, software and web development consultancies, professional services firms, and freelancers or small studios who bill clients hourly or need to monitor project profitability. Because its core mechanism is embedding into existing project management tools rather than replacing them, Everhour is particularly well suited to teams that already have an established workflow in Asana, Trello, Basecamp, Jira, or GitHub and want to add time tracking and budgeting without asking staff to adopt a second, disconnected app. This makes Everhour especially popular with software development teams and technical agencies that live inside Jira or GitHub issue trackers day to day.

In the competitive landscape, Everhour is most frequently compared to Toggl Track, Harvest, Clockify, TimeCamp, and Hubstaff. Relative to Toggl Track and Clockify, which emphasize simplicity and a standalone timer-first experience, Everhour differentiates through its deeper native embedding inside project management tools and its stronger budgeting and invoicing feature set aimed at agencies. Relative to Harvest, a long-established competitor with similar agency-billing positioning, Everhour is generally regarded as offering more granular per-task budget controls and tighter integrations with tools like Asana and Trello, though Harvest has broader brand recognition. Compared to Hubstaff, which leans more heavily into employee monitoring features like screenshots and activity levels, Everhour positions itself as a budgeting- and client-billing-first tool rather than an employee surveillance tool, appealing to teams that want time and budget visibility without monitoring-style features. Everhour holds a G2 rating in the high 4-out-of-5 range and has earned G2's 'Users Love Us' badge, along with a top-three ranking on the Asana Marketplace, reinforcing its reputation as a well-regarded, integration-first niche player rather than a category-leading, venture-scaled platform. The company's continued profitability and lack of outside funding, more than a decade after founding, is one of its most notable and frequently cited facts in press coverage and company materials, distinguishing it from many VC-backed SaaS competitors in the same space.

enrichment.seo_title: Everlaw Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Everlaw review 2026: see how this cloud-based e-discovery and litigation platform prices AI review, predictive coding and Storybuilder, plus top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Everlaw is a cloud-native software platform for electronic discovery (e-discovery), litigation, and investigations, built to help legal teams collect, review, analyze, and present large volumes of documents, emails, and other electronically stored information (ESI) in the course of litigation, government inquiries, and internal investigations. The platform combines a document review workspace with artificial intelligence tools, visual data analytics, legal hold management, redaction, deposition support, and a case-narrative builder called Storybuilder, all delivered through a web browser rather than on-premises software. It is used by law firms of all sizes, corporate legal and compliance departments, and government agencies, including the U.S. Department of Justice and multiple state attorneys general offices.

The company was founded by AJ Shankar, who holds an A.B. in Mathematics and Computer Science from Harvard and a Ph.D. in Computer Science from the University of California, Berkeley. Everlaw was established in Berkeley, California in 2011, growing out of Shankar's frustration with the clunky, outdated e-discovery tools he encountered as a litigation support engineer earlier in his career. The company later relocated its headquarters to Oakland, California, where it remains based today. Shankar continues to serve as CEO.

Everlaw's growth was fueled by a series of venture capital rounds. A Series B round raised 25 million dollars, led by Menlo Ventures with participation from existing investor Andreessen Horowitz. In March 2020, the company announced a 62 million dollar Series C round co-led by CapitalG, Alphabet's independent growth equity fund, and Menlo Ventures, with additional participation from Andreessen Horowitz and K9 Ventures. In November 2021, Everlaw announced a 202 million dollar Series D round led by TPG and HIG Growth Partners, with continued participation from CapitalG, Menlo Ventures, Andreessen Horowitz, and K9 Ventures. That round valued the company at more than 2 billion dollars, making Everlaw a legal-technology unicorn following a year in which the company reported roughly 80 percent year-over-year growth. Around the same period, Everlaw signed new contracts with AmLaw 50, 100, and 200 firms, government entities such as the Colorado State Attorney General's office, and corporations including Lime, Snowflake, and Coca-Cola Europe.

Technically, Everlaw is built as a multi-tenant cloud application hosted on Amazon Web Services, including AWS GovCloud for federal government clients, which allows it to scale document processing and search across large, distributed case data sets without customers needing to manage their own servers. Its review engine supports predictive coding and active learning, forms of technology-assisted review (TAR) that use machine learning to rank and prioritize documents by likely relevance, helping reviewers focus attention on the most important material first rather than reviewing a static random sample. In more recent years, Everlaw has layered generative AI capabilities on top of this foundation under the EverlawAI umbrella, which includes EverlawAI Assistant, EverlawAI Predictive Coding, and EverlawAI Clustering. The AI Assistant supports bulk document summarization, extraction of key names, dates, and figures, sentiment analysis, an on-demand document question-and-answer feature called Deep Dive that returns citations, a Writing Assistant that drafts statements of fact, arguments, and deposition summaries, and review coding suggestions with supporting rationale. Single-document AI actions and the Writing Assistant are included in the base subscription at no extra cost, while higher-volume batch AI operations such as bulk summarization, topic analysis, and translation are billed through purchased credits.

On security and compliance, Everlaw maintains SOC 2 Type II attestation covering security, availability, confidentiality, and privacy, along with ISO 27001 certification and UK Cyber Essentials Plus. For government customers, Everlaw has achieved FedRAMP Moderate Authorization for government-wide use and StateRAMP Moderate Authorization for state and local agencies, and it operates a dedicated instance on AWS GovCloud to meet federal data-residency and control requirements. Data is encrypted using AES-256 at rest and TLS 1.2 or higher in transit.

Everlaw's pricing model is usage-based rather than a fixed per-seat subscription. Instead of licensing individual users, the company charges based on the volume of data hosted on the platform, measured in gigabytes, combined with overall usage, and it markets this as having no limits on the number of users or uploads within a matter. Prospective customers must contact Everlaw's sales team for a quote, as the company does not publish fixed tier pricing; third-party market analyses estimate typical costs in the range of roughly 2,000 to 5,000 dollars per month in base fees plus per-gigabyte hosting charges, though actual costs vary significantly by data volume and contract terms. The core per-GB rate is described as bundling unlimited user licenses, standard data migration, onboarding and training, legal holds, processing and imaging, predictive coding, unlimited productions, audio and video transcription, Storybuilder, and cloud connectors for Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Slack, and Zoom, positioning the company's pricing as more predictable and all-inclusive than legacy per-user or per-module e-discovery licensing.

Everlaw's target audience spans three main segments: law firms ranging from small litigation boutiques to AmLaw 50, 100, and 200 firms; corporate legal and compliance departments handling internal investigations and regulatory matters; and government agencies, including the Department of Justice, state attorneys general offices such as those in Colorado and Ohio, and municipal legal offices such as the City of Minneapolis Attorney's Office. The company also works with the National Association of Attorneys General to bring modern e-discovery technology to state AG offices.

In the competitive e-discovery landscape, Everlaw is most frequently compared to Relativity, which remains the dominant market-share leader and the long-standing standard for the largest law firms and most complex litigation, as well as to DISCO, Reveal, Logikcull, Casepoint, and Nextpoint. Analysts generally position Everlaw, DISCO, and Reveal as cloud-native, AI-forward challengers to Relativity's more customizable but more complex legacy platform, with Everlaw specifically noted for its modern, consumer-grade interface, fast document processing and search, and its Storybuilder module for building chronological case narratives and preparing for depositions. Reviewers on G2 and Capterra consistently praise Everlaw's intuitive interface, quick search performance on large document sets, and responsive customer support, while citing a learning curve for configuring advanced search terms and highlights, along with occasional frustration around deduplication and identifying near-duplicate documents as recurring drawbacks.

Product development at Everlaw has continued at a steady cadence, with the company shipping regular platform releases that have added features such as Storybuilder Deposition Designations, deposition transcript search improvements, AI-generated coding suggestions for images, and the ability to redact specific cell content within native spreadsheets rather than requiring redaction of an entire cell. Everlaw also maintains a public API that lets organization administrators generate API keys or use OAuth2 to build custom integrations, automate reporting, track billing, and upload native data programmatically, along with direct integrations into third-party tools. Collectively, these investments in AI, security certifications, and integration capability have supported Everlaw's positioning as a modern alternative to legacy e-discovery software for organizations of varying size and sophistication.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Everlaw is a cloud-based e-discovery and litigation platform used to collect, review, analyze, and present electronically stored information in litigation, investigations, and regulatory matters. It was founded by AJ Shankar in 2011 and is headquartered in Oakland, California.

The platform serves three main audiences: law firms handling document-intensive litigation, corporate legal and compliance departments managing internal investigations, and government agencies such as the Department of Justice and state attorneys general offices that need secure, compliant hosting for sensitive case data.

Everlaw combines a document review workspace with AI-assisted search and predictive coding, visual case analytics, legal holds management, redaction tools, deposition support, and Storybuilder, a module for building chronological case narratives used in briefs and trial preparation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the core of Everlaw is a browser-based review workspace that supports predictive coding and active learning, forms of technology-assisted review that use machine learning to rank documents by likely relevance so reviewers can prioritize the most important material.

The EverlawAI suite adds generative AI capabilities, including single-document summaries, an on-demand question-and-answer tool called Deep Dive that cites its sources, a Writing Assistant that drafts statements of fact and deposition summaries, and AI-generated review coding suggestions with supporting rationale.

Storybuilder lets legal teams organize evidence into a visual, chronological case timeline for narrative development and trial or deposition preparation, while dedicated deposition tools support transcript search, synchronization with video, and AI-assisted transcript analysis.

Other included capabilities cover legal holds management, redaction (including redacting individual cells within native spreadsheets), unlimited productions, audio and video transcription, cloud connectors for Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Slack, and Zoom, and a public API for custom integrations and automation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Everlaw does not publish fixed subscription tiers. Instead, it uses a usage-based model priced primarily by the volume of data hosted on the platform, measured in gigabytes, with no limits on the number of users or document uploads within a matter.

Prospective customers need to contact Everlaw's sales team for a quote. Third-party market estimates put typical costs at roughly 2,000 to 5,000 dollars per month in base fees plus a per-gigabyte hosting rate, though actual pricing depends heavily on data volume, matter complexity, and contract terms.

The base subscription is positioned as all-inclusive, bundling unlimited user licenses, onboarding and training, legal holds, processing, predictive coding, Storybuilder, and single-document AI actions, while high-volume batch AI operations such as bulk summarization and translation are billed separately through purchased credits.
